cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The passage is a story where a man is settling accounts with his children's governess, Julia Vassilyevna. He starts by saying they agreed on 30 rubles a month, but she claims it was 40. He then deducts various amounts for Sundays, holidays, days when the kids were sick, her having a toothache, broken items, and other incidents. Eventually, he reduces her pay from 60 rubles (for two months) down to 14, then later corrects himself after she mentions she was only given 3 rubles before, leaving her with 11. However, he then gets angry that she's thanking him for the money and realizes he's cheated her. He ends up giving her 80 rubles and apologizes. The question asks why the employer made all those criticisms about Julia's work. The options are: A) she had neglected her duties. B) he wanted to pay her as little money as possible. C) he wanted to make her realize that she was being cheated and protest against it. D) he wanted to make her feel miserable. First, I need to understand the employer's motivation. Initially, the employer is deducting various amounts from her pay, but it seems like he might be doing this to test her. When he gives her the money and she thanks him, he gets angry because he realizes he's been unfair and wants her to stand up for herself. Then he ends up giving her the full 80 rubles. So his criticisms might have been a way to see if she would protest, to teach her a lesson about standing up for herself. Option C says he wanted to make her realize she was being cheated and protest. That seems to fit because after he gives her the money and she just says merci, he gets angry because she didn't
